Journal.Title: Canadian journal of public health = Revue canadienne de sante publique

Article.ArticleTitle: Beyond the reckoning: Addressing structural anti-Black racism in population and public health.

Abstract.AbstractText: The absence of historical context in public health has contributed to the persistent neglect of structural racism as a major determinant of health inequities. This commentary critically highlights the omissions of the Lalonde Report, a foundational document in population health, and explores the enduring impacts of slavery, colonialism, and systemic racism on Black health in Canada. Anti-Black racism continues to shape racial health inequities through economic, political, and social marginalization. However, public health research, policy, and practice have largely failed to address the structural dimensions of racism. Yet, Black communities in Canada have long resisted these injustices through grassroots movements, community advocacy, and systems transformation. A decolonial, anti-racist approach is necessary to disrupt these patterns, shift power and resources, and promote health equity. This requires interdisciplinary education, policy change, and investments in social systems that promote equity for Black communities. Centring community-led solutions, institutional accountability, and leveraging legislative tools are critical steps toward meaningful change. Public health must actively engage in dismantling white supremacy to achieve equitable health outcomes for Black communities.

AffiliationInfo.Affiliation: Black Health Education Collaborative, Dalla Lana School of Public Health, University of Toronto, Toronto, ON, Canada. sume.ndumbe.eyoh@utoronto.ca.

OtherAbstract.AbstractText: RéSUMé: L’absence de contexte historique en santé publique contribue au désintérêt persistant pour le racisme structurel en tant que déterminant majeur des iniquités en santé. Notre commentaire critique les omissions du rapport Lalonde, un document fondamental en santé des populations, et explore les effets durables de l’esclavage, du colonialisme et du racisme systémique sur la santé des personnes noires au Canada. Le racisme anti-Noirs continue de structurer les iniquités raciales en matière de santé par le biais d’une marginalisation économique, politique et sociale. Dans une large mesure toutefois, la recherche, les politiques et les pratiques en santé publique n’abordent pas les dimensions structurelles du racisme. Néanmoins, par les mouvements populaires, le plaidoyer communautaire et la transformation des systèmes, les communautés noires du Canada résistent depuis longtemps à ces injustices. Une approche décoloniale et antiraciste est nécessaire pour perturber ces schémas, déplacer le pouvoir et les ressources et promouvoir l’équité en santé. Elle exige une formation interdisciplinaire, un changement de cap et des investissements dans des systèmes sociaux qui favorisent l’équité pour les communautés noires. Un recentrage sur les solutions communautaires, une responsabilisation des institutions et l’utilisation d’instruments législatifs sont les étapes essentielles d’un véritable changement. La santé publique doit activement participer au démantèlement de la suprématie blanche pour obtenir des résultats équitables pour la santé des communautés noires.

MedlineCitation.CoiStatement: Declarations. Consent to participate: N/A. Consent for publication: N/A. Conflict of interest: The author declares no competing interests.

Reference.Citation: Agénor, M., Perkins, C., Stamoulis, C., Hall, R. D., Samnaliev, M., Berland, S., & Austin, S. B. (2021). Developing a database of structural racism–related state laws for health equity research and practice in the United States. Public Health Reports. 10.1177/0033354920984168

Alang, S., Hardeman, R., Karbeah, J., Akosionu, O., McGuire, C., Abdi, H., & McAlpine, D. (2021). White supremacy and the core functions of public health. American Journal of Public Health,111(5), 815–819. 10.2105/AJPH.2020.306137
Allan, B. (2022). Theorizing decolonizing equity. In V. C. Rhonda. Hackett & Billie. Allan (Eds.), Decolonizing equity (pp. 19–39). Fernwood Publishing.

Banting, K., & Thompson, D. (2021). The puzzling persistence of racial inequality in Canada. Canadian Journal of Political Science = Revue Canadienne De Science Politique,54(4), 870–891. 10.1017/S0008423921000585
hooks, b. (2003). Teaching community: A pedagogy of hope. Routledge.
Lalonde, M. (1974). A new perspective on the health of Canadians. https://www.phac-aspc.gc.ca/ph-sp/pdf/perspect-eng.pdf. Accessed 12 Dec 2024.
Maynard, R. (2017). Policing Black lives: State violence in Canada from slavery to the present. Fernwood Publishing.
Ndumbe-Eyoh, S. (2020). What would it take for health promotion to take structural racism seriously? In Global Health Promotion,27(4), 3–5. 10.1177/1757975920972259

Public Health Agency of Canada. (2020). Social determinants and inequities in health for Black Canadians: A snapshot. https://www.canada.ca/en/public-health/services/health-promotion/population-health/what-determines-health/social-determinants-inequities-black-canadians-snapshot.html. Accessed 12 Dec 2024.

Rakotovao, L., Simeoni, M., Bennett-AbuAyyash, C., Walji, T., & Abdi, S. (2024). Addressing anti-Black racism within public health in North America: A scoping review. International Journal for Equity in Health,23(1), 128. 10.1186/S12939-024-02124-4
Scott-Samuel, A., & Smith, K. E. (2015). Fantasy paradigms of health inequalities: Utopian thinking? Social Theory & Health,13, 418–436. 10.1057/sth.2015.12
The Canadian Coalition for Environmental and Climate Justice. (2022). National environmental justice strategy. https://ccecj.ca/work/national-environmental-justice-strategy/. Accessed 12 Dec 2024.

Williams, D. R., & Cooper, L. A. (2019). Reducing racial inequities in health: Using what we already know to take action. International Journal of Environmental Research and Public Health,16(4), Article 606. 10.3390/ijerph16040606
